format: In-class exam with calculations and strategy analysis

questions.questionText:
Factory Physics Problem: Station A | Station B | Station C | Station D | Whole Process | Total Batch Size

Setup time [min]: 1 | 1 | 5 | 3 | 50 | 85
Processing time per unit [min]: 0.8 | 4 | 6.8 | 3
Number of workers: 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 4

Calculate capacities, identify bottleneck, determine flow rate and utilizations for both the original batch size and a different batch size scenario.
questions.solutionText:
Original Scenario:
Total time per batch [min]: 13 | 11 | 15 | 13 | 61 | 45 | 52 | 7
Capacity [batches/hour]: 0.41
Capacity [units/hour]: 8.28
Process capacity [units/hr]: 8.28 (limited by bottleneck)
Demand rate [units/hr]: 6.00
Flow rate [units/hr]: 6.00 (demand-constrained)
Utilization: varies by station

Key Solution Points:
1. Capacities for each activity are calculated in batches per hour and units per hour.
2. Station D is the bottleneck.
3. The capacity of the facility equals the capacity of the bottleneck.
4. The flow rate is 6 units/hr - demand-constrained.
5. Utilizations are calculated for each station.

With Different Batch Size:
Total time per batch [min]: 12 | 37 | 56 | 81 | 15 | 38 | 1
Capacity [batches/hour]: 0.49 | 0.80 | 0.88 | 0.52 | 0.49
Capacity [units/hour]: 4.88
Process capacity [units/hr]: 4.88
Demand rate [units/hr]: 6.00
Flow rate [units/hr]: 4.88 (capacity-constrained)

7. Station A is the bottleneck.
8. The flow rate equals the capacity of the bottleneck (Station A).
9. Station C utilization is calculated.
10. Smaller batch size means more setup time, and therefore time lost. So the facility capacity decreases.
11. Optimal batch size is about 12 or 13 units (using Goal Seek or trial and error).
keyInsights: Bottleneck shifts when batch sizes change
Smaller batch sizes increase setup frequency and reduce effective capacity
System can be either demand-constrained or capacity-constrained

questions.questionText:
Operations Strategy Question for AA company:

Objective Definition – How does AA define each objective?
Measurements – How does, or could, AA measure the objective?
Rank – How would you rank each objective for AA

Part 2: AA is considering a shift to a completely different business model with high customization and low volume. Analyze the implications.
questions.solutionText:
CQDF Analysis for AA:

Cost: Competitive with other manufacturers. $/unit
Rank: 3 = just competitive.

Quality: Very high. Reputation. Never ship defective product. Probably # of defects. # of defects shipped. Quality right the first time.
Rank: 1 = higher than delivery, due to never ship defectives.

Delivery: Very tight windows; quarterly reports on late deliveries. Make-to-stock. # and % of late deliveries. % meet window targets.
Rank: 2 = key issue now; 2nd relative to Quality.

Flexibility: Number of SKUs; low frequency of new product introduction. 200 SKUs – not high in this industry
Rank: 4 = could be higher than cost, but this is better.

Part 2 Major Points:
• This would be a completely different business and operational model. Different operations objectives with the new proposal. Flexibility would be now #1 or 2, along with quality. More customization; many new products, rather than a more standardized, high volume product line.

• Production in Costa Rica is high volume, not low volume. Different place on the product-process matrix. They would be shifting from a Beleza-type model to a traditional salon type model. Although that may lead to higher margins, it's completely different on the "right side of the board" because they have changed the "left side of the board."

• Current low production is in US, but that facility will close. Other key points:
• There is no way to introduce new products in Costa Rica; current new product development is in the US and they would close that facility.
• Abandon current customers. Different sales team and sales cycle.
keyInsights: Must define, rank, and measure each CQDF dimension
AA prioritizes Quality #1, Delivery #2, Cost #3, Flexibility #4
Changing business model requires completely different operational priorities
Product-process matrix alignment is critical - can't have high volume AND high customization

questions.questionText:
Grohe Lux Problem:

Before implementation:
WIP: 7500
Throughput time: 4.3
Throughput Rate: 1744

After implementation:
WIP: 1200 
Throughput time: 1
Throughput Rate: 1200

Part 2: Explain the relationship between WIP reduction and the observed changes in throughput time and throughput rate.
questions.solutionText:
Given data:
                Before    After
WIP             7500      1200
Throughput time 4.3       1
Throughput Rate 1744      1200

Part 2 Analysis:
Recall the graphs from the Factory Physics class. Reducing WIP reduces throughput time, which is what they observed. However, it also reduces throughput rate, which means that they are not able to sell as many products and thus lose revenue. "Buffer or Suffer." 

It can help to draw the curves and see how they are nonlinear, with stronger effects at certain levels of WIP.

This demonstrates the fundamental tradeoff in Factory Physics: you can optimize for throughput time (customer experience) OR throughput rate (revenue), but not both simultaneously. Lower WIP means faster flow but less total output.
keyInsights: WIP reduction improves throughput time but can reduce throughput rate
"Buffer or Suffer" - fundamental tradeoff in Factory Physics
Cannot optimize both throughput time and throughput rate simultaneously
Factory Physics curves show nonlinear relationships between WIP, time, and rate

keyFormulas: Little's Law: WIP = Throughput Rate × Throughput Time
